text:
Code was amended to introduce new protections. 156 Be that as it may, today protections

for privacy are a proud part of the law in both countries—and in both, they have retained

their nineteenth-century coloration. 157 To be sure, the law of privacy in both countries is

today regarded as distinct from the law of insult. 158 Nevertheless, traditional nineteenth-

century values, with their honor-oriented, suspicious attitude toward the free press and

the free market, have continued to make themselves felt, even in a world in which the

privacy of ordinary folks is vigorously protected.

The continental chariness about the free market shows, for example, in the

treatment of consumer credit reporting and other consumer data. Credit reporting is an

especially revealing example. Here the basic continental rules grow out of longstanding

continental traditions. Historically, as a matter of etiquette, one’s financial affairs were

very much one’s own affairs. One did not talk about money matters unless absolutely

necessary: Indeed, money, as a standard etiquette guide will tell you, is simply “a taboo

subject” among respectable people. 159 The only persons whose finances were routinely

revealed to the public were insolvents and bankrupts. That attitude has had a notable

influence on European privacy law—most notably in the traditional French rule that

155
    Ordonnance du 6 mai 1944, in Gazette du Palais 1944, vol. 2, p. 292, amending the Loi du 29 Juillet
1881. For the limited impact on post-war law, see the discussion of Robert Badinter, Le droit au respect de
de la vie privée in La Semaine Juridique (=Juriclasseur Périodique) 1968, I: 2136, paragraph 10.
Nevertheless, it is striking how infrequently this ordonnance is mentioned in the literature.
156
    Art. 9, Code Civil.
157
    For the continuing vigor of the law of insult in France, see Lamberterie and Strubel, L’Image
Manipulée, 356.
158
    Bertrand, Droit à la vie privée, 17-18.
159
    Le Bras, Guide du Savoir-Vivre, 66.

52
 made it a per se violation of “privacy” rights to reveal another person’s salary. 160

(Indeed, a French text on the law of privacy will still casually list “love, sex and

earnings” as the three areas of life self-evidently in need of privacy protections. 161 ) The

same attitude has had an influence on the continental law of credit reporting. In France in

particular, consumer credit reports are provided only by official sources, and they are

provided only in the case of persons experiencing serious financial difficulty. They offer,

as it were, only a watch list of persons who are proven credit risks. Anything else, to the

French mind, would represent a intrusion into financial privacy. 162 One’s financial

information is information “of a personal character,” over which one must have control

just as one must have control over one’s image. 163 German norms are a little more
